paper_title: Economic Growth and Complexity in the UAE: Summary Report
research_answer: The two periods represent a striking reversal. Between 2005 and 2012, essentially all of the UAE's export sectors — oil, non-oil goods, re-exports, and services — saw robust growth. Much of the non-oil goods momentum in that period was led by stone exports, with chemicals and metals also growing strongly from a low base. But between 2012 and 2016, oil, non-oil goods, and re-exports all fell or stagnated. Services were the exception — they maintained steady growth throughout the entire 2005–2019 period without the same boom-bust pattern. Post-2012, most non-oil goods sectors have remained more or less flat, suggesting that the earlier growth was partly tied to the commodity and construction boom rather than a durable structural shift in the UAE's export capabilities.
supporting_quotes: From 2005 to 2012, all export sectors saw robust growth. Between 2012 and 2016, oil, non-oil, and re-exports all fell or stagnated.
Much of this growth has been concentrated in stone exports, especially between 2005 and 2012.
There has also been strong growth (albeit from a low base) in chemicals and metals, again the bulk of this growth coming between 2005 and 2012.
Post 2011-2012, we can see that most non-oil goods sector exports have more or less stagnated.
Non-oil non-re-exports are still stagnant overall. On the other hand, services exports have seen steady and consistent growth during the entire period.
